California
Fair Political
Practices Cornrnission
June 1, 199 0
Honorable chris chandler
Assembl)aran, Third D j-strict
State Capitol
P. o. Box 942449
Sacramento, CA 94249-OOO1
Re:
Your Request for Advice
our Fil-e No. A-9o-374
Dear Mr. chandLer:
This letter is in response to your request for advi.ce regard-
ing the roass mailing provisions of the Polj.tica] Refonu Act (the
nalgr 1 . 1
OUESTION
Does a mass mailing to a se]-ect group of students in your
district 
violate the provisions of the Act?
CONCLUS ION
A mass maiJ.ing to a select group of students in your district
does not violate the provisions of the Act if the nailing reaches
the students in their classrooms and is not sent to their homes.
FACTS
The Department of Education recognized approximatefy 64o
students attendj.ng schools in your district 
for their outstanding
achievement in certain acadenic subjects. The Department of
Education forwarded the names of these students to you. You wish
to send individual letters to these students congratulating then
1 colr"rrrr"nt code Sections SLooo-91o15. A11 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
commission
regulatj.ons appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seq. AII references to regTulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations-
-128 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 . Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 122'5660


our liLe No.
Paqe 2
A-90-374
for their outstandingf performance. The letters vroul-d be sent to
the schools in your district 
where these students are enrolled.
Tfre teachers would distribute the letters to the students in the
classroom. You have forvarded for our review a copy of the Letter
you intend to send out (copy enclosed). This sample letter is
drafted on letterhead which includes your name and titLe. 
Your
nane and title 
also are printed at the end of the letter. 
An ap-
propriate space is provided for your signature.
ANALYSIS
section 89oo1 provides that no ne$rsletter or other rnass mail-
ing shall be sent at public expense. The Act def j.nes a rrmass
nailingtt as more than 2oo substantially similar pieces of naif
sent in a caLendar uonth. (Section 82041.5.)
A literat 
reading of section 89001 $rould lead to the conclu-
sion that aIl publicly-funded mass mailings are prohibited.
However, the comruission adopted-Regulation 18901 to clarify which
uailings are indeed prohibited.2 A mass rnailing is prohj-bited
only if alL of the following aPPIY:
(1) The j-ten is deLivered, by any means, to the recipient at
hi.s or her residence, Place of enployment or business, or post
office box;
(2) The j.tem sent was either prepared or sent in co-
operati,on, consultation, coordi.nation, or concert with the elected
oificer who is affiliated 
with the agency and includes the
officer's 
namef office, photograph, or some other reference to the
elected officer,' or, the item features an elected off j-cer who is
affiliated 
with the agency which produced or sent the mailing;
(3) The cost of distributing the j.tem is paid for with
public funds; or more than S5o in public funds was spent in the
aesign or production of the itern and the iten was designed or
produced rrith the intent of sending the iten; or
(4) !{ore than 20b copies of the same or substantially
sinilar iterns are sent in a single calendar rnonth. (ReguLation
18901. (a) , copy enclosed. )
You intend to naiL or otherwise distribute more than 200 let-
ters j.n one calendar nonth. This constitutes a nass mai-1ing.
Moreover, the cost of distribution will be paid with public funds
and the letters $/iLl feature you, an elected officiaL, 
by display-
ing your signature. 
(Regrulation 1s901(c) (2).) 
This nass maiJ-ing
would be prohibited if the letters were delivered to the students
2 Th" Commission's authority to interpret section SgooL to
the absurd results of its Iiteral application \.ras upheld in
v. Fair Political Practices 
, case No. 8042250, Second
District court of AppeaL, 90 D.A.R. 1312 (February 6, 1990)
avo id
Watson


at their residence/ place of emplo)rment or business, or post of-
fice, or if the letters were dlrected to the parents of the
students and the students were a means of delivery to the parents'
homes. However, you intend to send the letters to the schools
where the students are enrolled. 
The letters are addressed to the
stud.ents and not to their parents and will be distributed to the
students by their teachers. Accordingly, the 1etters.lriIl 
not be
delj.vered Lo the intended recipient at his or her residence, place
of emplolment or business, or post office and are thus not
prohibited mass mailings.
Since the materials would be prohibited nass mailings were
they to be sent to the students' homes, we reconmend that you
include a cover letter \.rith the bulk delivery to each school mak-
ing it clear that the letters are to be delivered to the students
in school premises and are not to be redistributed by nailing or
othervrise sending the tetters dj.rectly to the students' homes'
(See kyarcllg 
Advice Letter, our File No. A-90-199, copy
enclosed. )
We trust this letter adequatel-y responds to your inquiry'
should you have any further questions regarding this natter, do
not hesitate to call- me at (916) 322-5901.
Sincerelyâ€²
Scott Halâ– abrin
Acting Ceneraâ–  COunseâ– 
Our Fiâ– e No.
Page 3

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a mass mailing to a se]-ect group of students in your
district 
violate the provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION: A mass maiJ.ing to a select group of students in your district
does not violate the provisions of the Act if the nailing reaches
the students in their classrooms and is not sent to their homes.
